narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background and achievements—like your solid LSAT score of 37, which is right around the typical student's 36.7—have been carefully reviewed by our admissions model, which predicts performance on the bar exam. The model estimates a 61.5% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application to help ensure we admit students likely to succeed. This prediction hinges mostly on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, with your Year 3 decile of 2—well below the average of about 5.5—standing out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than the typical 5.7, reinforced this concern as the third-most influential element. On a brighter note, your LSAT score was the second-biggest positive influence, pulling the prediction toward success and showing your potential in high-stakes testing. Other details, like your middle family income bracket and plan to attend full-time like most applicants, played smaller roles. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your earlier grades place you in the lower deciles, which the model sees as the biggest red flags for bar exam struggles. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 is a bit below the average 3.21, it wasn't as pivotal here. This isn't a judgment on your character or drive—many from similar backgrounds overcome these hurdles—but the model prioritizes patterns from past students. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted coursework or retakes, perhaps highlighting any upward trends since Year 3. Consider retaking the LSAT to push that strength even further, or gathering letters that speak to your resilience as a Hispanic student navigating these challenges. With these steps, you can reapply stronger and show the model—and schools—what you're truly capable of.
